}

######################################################################
# Group declaration (category metadata for context generation)

# buz_group() - Declare a colophon group category
# Args: constant_name, prefix, description
# Stores group metadata at the current roll position for buz_emit_context.
buz_group() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local -r z_constant="${1:-}"
  local -r z_prefix="${2:-}"
  local -r z_description="${3:-}"
  test -n "${z_constant}"    || buc_die "buz_group: constant name required"
  test -n "${z_prefix}"      || buc_die "buz_group: prefix required"
  test -n "${z_description}" || buc_die "buz_group: description required"

  z_buz_group_index_roll+=("${#z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}")
  z_buz_group_prefix_roll+=("${z_prefix}")
  z_buz_group_description_roll+=("${z_description}")
}

######################################################################
# Public enroll (kindle-only registry population)

# buz_enroll() - Register colophon tuple in parallel rolls
# Args: varname, colophon, module, command, channel, description
# All 6 arguments required. Channel: "" (none), "imprint", or "param1".
# Assigns colophon string to caller's variable via printf -v
# Side effects: populates registry rolls (must be called in same process, NOT inside $())
buz_enroll() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  test $# -eq 6 || buc_die "buz_enroll: requires 6 arguments (varname colophon module command channel description), got $#"

  local -r z_varname="${1}"
  local -r z_colophon="${2}"
  local -r z_module="${3}"
  local -r z_command="${4}"
  local -r z_channel="${5}"
  local -r z_description="${6}"
  test -n "${z_varname}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: varname required"
  test -n "${z_colophon}"    || buc_die "buz_enroll: colophon required"
  test -n "${z_module}"      || buc_die "buz_enroll: module required"
  test -n "${z_command}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: command required"
  test -n "${z_description}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: description required"

  # Validate channel value (empty string is valid — means no channel)
  case "${z_channel}" in
    ""|"imprint"|"param1") ;;
    *) buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid channel: ${z_channel}" ;;
  esac

  # Validate variable name
  [[ "${z_varname}" =~ ^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$ ]] \
    || buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid variable name: ${z_varname}"

  # Roll population (only persists in same-process context, lost in $() subshell)
  z_buz_colophon_roll+=("${z_colophon}")
  z_buz_module_roll+=("${z_module}")
  z_buz_command_roll+=("${z_command}")
  z_buz_channel_roll+=("${z_channel}")
  z_buz_describe_roll+=("${z_description}")

  # Assign colophon to caller's variable
  printf -v "${z_varname}" '%s' "${z_colophon}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: printf -v failed for ${z_varname}"
}

######################################################################
# Context generation (emit markdown from registry metadata)

# buz_emit_context() - Emit colophon registry as markdown, organized by groups
# Args: tabtarget_dir
# Writes markdown to stdout. Groups come from buz_group; descriptions from buz_enroll.
# Frontispiece extracted from tabtarget filenames on disk.
buz_emit_context() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local -r z_tt_dir="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_tt_dir}" || buc_die "buz_emit_context: tabtarget directory required"

  printf '%s\n' "## Command Reference (Generated)"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  printf '%s\n' "<!-- Generated by buz_emit_context from zipper registry. Do not edit. -->"
  printf '%s\n' "<!-- Regenerate: tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh -->"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  printf '%s\n' "**Folio** is the runtime target value passed to a command (nameplate moniker, role name, etc.)."
  printf '%s\n' "The Folio column shows how each tabtarget receives it:"
  printf '%s\n' ""

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Stanza English UD pipeline wrapper.

Per A9AAV guidance: emit all available Stanza outputs at the token level
(upos, xpos, feats, lemma, head, deprel, BIO ner tag) plus aggregated
entity spans. Combining can decide later which signals matter.

Findings shape per APCPS Stanza schema:
    {"kind": "token",  "text", "start", "end", "upos", "xpos", "feats", "lemma", "head", "deprel", "ner"}
    {"kind": "entity", "text", "start", "end", "label"}
"""

import stanza


_pipeline = stanza.Pipeline(
    lang="en",
    dir="/opt/models/stanza",
    processors="tokenize,pos,lemma,depparse,ner",
    download_method=None,
    use_gpu=False,
    verbose=False,
)


def analyze(text):
    if not text:
        return []

    doc = _pipeline(text)
    findings = []

    for sent in doc.sentences:
        for word in sent.words:
            # word.parent is the Token containing this Word; for non-MWT
            # English data, word and parent line up 1:1.
            parent = word.parent
            findings.append({
                "kind": "token",
                "text": word.text,
                "start": parent.start_char,
                "end": parent.end_char,
                "upos": word.upos or "",
                "xpos": word.xpos or "",
                "feats": word.feats or "",
                "lemma": word.lemma or "",
                "head": word.head,
                "deprel": word.deprel or "",
                "ner": parent.ner or "O",
            })

    for ent in doc.entities:
        findings.append({
            "kind": "entity",
            "text": ent.text,
            "start": ent.start_char,
            "end": ent.end_char,
            "label": ent.type,
        })

    return findings

<!--

SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
See LICENSE for terms.
-->

# APCK Container

Long-running Python container that runs three NLP discerners
(`apcs_stanford_scan`, `apcs_spacy_scan`, `apcs_stanza_scan`) against
normalized clipboard text staged by Clipbuddy. See APCPS Container
Architecture for the conceptual contract.

The container is **decoupled from Clipbuddy**: it runs as a long-lived
process started by the developer (or, eventually, a system service on
Ann's machine), watches `$HOME/apcjd/` for `{N}-in.txt` files, and writes
consolidated `{N}.json` outputs back into the same directory.

## Lifecycle Tabtargets

| Tabtarget | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `tt/apcw-cb.ContainerBuild.sh` | `docker build` the image (first build pulls ML models — 5–10 min) |
| `tt/apcw-cs.ContainerStart.sh` | Truncate `container-log.txt`, `docker run` with `--network=none --cap-drop=all --read-only` |
| `tt/apcw-cx.ContainerStop.sh` | `docker stop` + `docker rm` |
| `tt/apcw-ci.ContainerStatus.sh` | Container running state, image, bind-mount reachability, last log lines |

## Smoke Test

Verifies the wire contract end-to-end: image builds, container starts,
input file is consumed, all three discerners produce findings, output
file appears atomically.

1. **Build the image:**
   ```
   tt/apcw-cb.ContainerBuild.sh
   ```
   First run takes 5–10 min — torch CPU wheel + transformers + scispaCy
   model + Stanza English UD package are all pulled from upstream during
   the build so the runtime container can run with `--network=none`.

2. **Start the container:**
   ```
   tt/apcw-cs.ContainerStart.sh
   ```
   Container detaches; model load takes a further 30–90s before
   discerners are ready. Watch `~/apcjd/container-log.txt` for the
   `loading stanford → loading spacy → loading stanza → ready` sequence.

3. **Stage a normalized input:**
   ```
   printf 'Patient: Margaret Thornton. DOB: 03/15/1952. Seen at Mercy General by Dr. Chen.' \
     > ~/apcjd/10000-in.txt
   ```

4. **Wait for the output (up to 30s after `ready`):**
   ```
   for i in {1..30}; do
     test -f ~/apcjd/10000.json && break
     sleep 1
   done
   test -f ~/apcjd/10000.json && echo "OK" || echo "TIMEOUT"
   ```

5. **Validate the JSON shape:**
   ```
   python3 -c "
   import json
   d = json.load(open('$HOME/apcjd/10000.json'))
   assert d['index'] == 10000
   for k in ('stanford', 'spacy', 'stanza'):
       assert k in d, f'missing {k}'
       assert 'findings' in d[k], f'missing {k}.findings'
       assert isinstance(d[k]['findings'], list), f'{k}.findings not a list'
       print(f'{k}: {len(d[k][\"findings\"])} findings')
   "
   ```
   Expected: each discerner reports a non-zero finding count. Stanford
   should label `Margaret Thornton` as `PATIENT` and `Mercy General` as
   `HOSPITAL`.

6. **Verify no temp file remains:**
   ```
   test ! -f ~/apcjd/10000.json.tmp && echo "OK: no leftover .tmp"
   ```

7. **Verify the log is populated:**
   ```
   grep -E 'starting|ready|processed index=10000' ~/apcjd/container-log.txt
   ```

8. **Stop the container:**
   ```
   tt/apcw-cx.ContainerStop.sh
   ```

## Wire Format

See APCPS § Container Architecture / Wire Protocol and § Container
Output JSON Schema for the authoritative definition. Briefly:

- Inputs: `{N}-in.txt` — UTF-8 normalized text.
- Outputs: `{N}.json` — top-level `{"index": N, "stanford": {"findings": [...]}, "spacy": {...}, "stanza": {...}}`.
- Atomicity: the container writes `{N}.json.tmp` then `os.rename` to
  `{N}.json`. Readers see either the absent state or the final file.
- Highest-N policy: only the highest-indexed `{N}-in.txt` is processed;
  lower indices are ignored (drop-old backlog).

## Security Posture

Enforced by `apcw-cs`:

- `--network=none` — no network namespace; PHI cannot exfiltrate.
- `--cap-drop=all` — no Linux capabilities.
- `--read-only` — root filesystem is immutable; only the bind mount is writable.
- `--tmpfs /tmp` — Python needs a writable `/tmp` for some library transients.
- `--user nobody:nogroup` — non-root runtime.
- `-v $HOME/apcjd:/work/apcjd` — only the journal directory is bind-mounted.
- `-e APCS_BINDMOUNT=/work/apcjd` — entrypoint reads this to find its workspace.

content.text: Build failed at the torch install — the CPU index serves `2.0–2.5.1` without the `+cpu` suffix (only 2.6+ uses `+cpu`). Fixing.

input.new_string:
# Pinned to 2.5.1 — last release on the PyTorch CPU index that uses the
# bare version (no '+cpu' suffix). 2.6.0+ switched to '+cpu'-suffixed wheels;
# either family works, but 2.5.1 has the broadest transformers/scispaCy
# compatibility window.
RUN pip install --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu torch==2.5.1

toolUseResult.newString:
# Pinned to 2.5.1 — last release on the PyTorch CPU index that uses the
# bare version (no '+cpu' suffix). 2.6.0+ switched to '+cpu'-suffixed wheels;
# either family works, but 2.5.1 has the broadest transformers/scispaCy
# compatibility window.
RUN pip install --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu torch==2.5.1
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# APCK container — Python 3.11-slim base + transformers/PyTorch CPU + scispaCy + Stanza.
#
# Models are pre-fetched during build so the runtime container can run with
# --network=none. First build is slow (~5–10 min) because of the model pulls;
# the layer cache makes incremental builds quick.

FROM python:3.11-slim AS base

# Locale-stable, no .pyc litter, unbuffered stdio.
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1 \
    PIP_DISABLE_PIP_VERSION_CHECK=1 \
    HF_HOME=/opt/models/hf \
    TRANSFORMERS_CACHE=/opt/models/hf \
    STANZA_RESOURCES_DIR=/opt/models/stanza

# Build-time tools (none beyond curl for the en_core_sci_md wheel pull). All
# wheels are binary — no compiler needed.
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        curl \
        ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /opt/apck

# Install Python deps in two phases so the heavy torch wheel is its own layer.
RUN pip install --upgrade pip
RUN pip install --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu torch==2.3.1+cpu

COPY requirements.txt /opt/apck/requirements.txt
RUN pip install -r /opt/apck/requirements.txt

# scispaCy model (en_core_sci_md @ 0.5.4 — the version published against
# scispaCy 0.5.5 / spaCy 3.7).
RUN pip install \
    https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/ai2-s2-scispacy/releases/v0.5.4/en_core_sci_md-0.5.4.tar.gz

# Pre-fetch HuggingFace + Stanza models into image layers so runtime needs no network.
RUN python -c "from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForTokenClassification; \
m='StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base'; \
AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(m); AutoModelForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(m)"

RUN python -c "import stanza; stanza.download('en', dir='/opt/models/stanza', processors='tokenize,pos,lemma,depparse,ner')"

# Application sources last — change-frequency low layers above, high below.
COPY entrypoint.py /opt/apck/entrypoint.py
COPY discerners /opt/apck/discerners

# Non-root runtime user. Pre-existing 'nobody' UID 65534 / GID 65534 keeps
# bind-mount file ownership stable. Models live under /opt/models — owned by
# root but world-readable, fine for inference.
RUN chmod -R a+rX /opt/apck /opt/models

USER nobody:nogroup

ENTRYPOINT ["python", "/opt/apck/entrypoint.py"]

input.old_string:
# Build-time tools (none beyond curl for the en_core_sci_md wheel pull). All
# wheels are binary — no compiler needed.
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        curl \
        ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
input.new_string:
# curl + ca-certificates for the en_core_sci_md wheel pull; build-essential
# because scispaCy 0.5.5 depends on nmslib-metabrainz, which has no prebuilt
# arm64 wheel and must compile from source. Build-essential stays in the
# image — kept simple for prototype; multistage trim is a Future-Brad task.
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        curl \
        ca-certificates \
        build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

toolUseResult.newString:
# curl + ca-certificates for the en_core_sci_md wheel pull; build-essential
# because scispaCy 0.5.5 depends on nmslib-metabrainz, which has no prebuilt
# arm64 wheel and must compile from source. Build-essential stays in the

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# APCK container — Python 3.11-slim base + transformers/PyTorch CPU + scispaCy + Stanza.
#
# Models are pre-fetched during build so the runtime container can run with
# --network=none. First build is slow (~5–10 min) because of the model pulls;
# the layer cache makes incremental builds quick.

FROM python:3.11-slim AS base

# Locale-stable, no .pyc litter, unbuffered stdio.
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1 \
    PIP_DISABLE_PIP_VERSION_CHECK=1 \
    HF_HOME=/opt/models/hf \
    TRANSFORMERS_CACHE=/opt/models/hf \
    STANZA_RESOURCES_DIR=/opt/models/stanza

# Build-time tools (none beyond curl for the en_core_sci_md wheel pull). All
# wheels are binary — no compiler needed.
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        curl \
        ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /opt/apck

# Install Python deps in two phases so the heavy torch wheel is its own layer.
RUN pip install --upgrade pip
# Pinned to 2.5.1 — last release on the PyTorch CPU index that uses the
# bare version (no '+cpu' suffix). 2.6.0+ switched to '+cpu'-suffixed wheels;
# either family works, but 2.5.1 has the broadest transformers/scispaCy
# compatibility window.
RUN pip install --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu torch==2.5.1

COPY requirements.txt /opt/apck/requirements.txt
RUN pip install -r /opt/apck/requirements.txt

# scispaCy model (en_core_sci_md @ 0.5.4 — the version published against
# scispaCy 0.5.5 / spaCy 3.7).
RUN pip install \
    https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/ai2-s2-scispacy/releases/v0.5.4/en_core_sci_md-0.5.4.tar.gz

# Pre-fetch HuggingFace + Stanza models into image layers so runtime needs no network.
RUN python -c "from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForTokenClassification; \
m='StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base'; \
AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(m); AutoModelForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(m)"

RUN python -c "import stanza; stanza.download('en', dir='/opt/models/stanza', processors='tokenize,pos,lemma,depparse,ner')"

# Application sources last — change-frequency low layers above, high below.
COPY entrypoint.py /opt/apck/entrypoint.py
COPY discerners /opt/apck/discerners

# Non-root runtime user. Pre-existing 'nobody' UID 65534 / GID 65534 keeps
# bind-mount file ownership stable. Models live under /opt/models — owned by
# root but world-readable, fine for inference.
RUN chmod -R a+rX /opt/apck /opt/models

USER nobody:nogroup

ENTRYPOINT ["python", "/opt/apck/entrypoint.py"]

# Download dir is governed by STANZA_RESOURCES_DIR env var (set above).
# stanza.download()'s explicit param is `model_dir`; stanza.Pipeline()'s is
# `dir` — different names for the same concept. Env var sidesteps both.
RUN python -c "import stanza; stanza.download('en', processors='tokenize,pos,lemma,depparse,ner')"

toolUseResult.newString:
# Download dir is governed by STANZA_RESOURCES_DIR env var (set above).
# stanza.download()'s explicit param is `model_dir`; stanza.Pipeline()'s is
# `dir` — different names for the same concept. Env var sidesteps both.
RUN python -c "import stanza; stanza.download('en', processors='tokenize,pos,lemma,depparse,ner')"
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# APCK container — Python 3.11-slim base + transformers/PyTorch CPU + scispaCy + Stanza.
#
# Models are pre-fetched during build so the runtime container can run with
# --network=none. First build is slow (~5–10 min) because of the model pulls;
# the layer cache makes incremental builds quick.

FROM python:3.11-slim AS base

# Locale-stable, no .pyc litter, unbuffered stdio.
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1 \
    PIP_DISABLE_PIP_VERSION_CHECK=1 \
    HF_HOME=/opt/models/hf \
    TRANSFORMERS_CACHE=/opt/models/hf \
    STANZA_RESOURCES_DIR=/opt/models/stanza

# curl + ca-certificates for the en_core_sci_md wheel pull; build-essential
# because scispaCy 0.5.5 depends on nmslib-metabrainz, which has no prebuilt
# arm64 wheel and must compile from source. Build-essential stays in the
# image — kept simple for prototype; multistage trim is a Future-Brad task.
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        curl \
        ca-certificates \
        build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /opt/apck

# Install Python deps in two phases so the heavy torch wheel is its own layer.
RUN pip install --upgrade pip
# Pinned to 2.5.1 — last release on the PyTorch CPU index that uses the
# bare version (no '+cpu' suffix). 2.6.0+ switched to '+cpu'-suffixed wheels;
# either family works, but 2.5.1 has the broadest transformers/scispaCy
# compatibility window.
RUN pip install --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu torch==2.5.1

COPY requirements.txt /opt/apck/requirements.txt
RUN pip install -r /opt/apck/requirements.txt

# scispaCy model (en_core_sci_md @ 0.5.4 — the version published against
# scispaCy 0.5.5 / spaCy 3.7).
RUN pip install \
    https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/ai2-s2-scispacy/releases/v0.5.4/en_core_sci_md-0.5.4.tar.gz

# Pre-fetch HuggingFace + Stanza models into image layers so runtime needs no network.
RUN python -c "from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForTokenClassification; \
m='StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base'; \
AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(m); AutoModelForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(m)"

RUN python -c "import stanza; stanza.download('en', dir='/opt/models/stanza', processors='tokenize,pos,lemma,depparse,ner')"

# Application sources last — change-frequency low layers above, high below.
COPY entrypoint.py /opt/apck/entrypoint.py
COPY discerners /opt/apck/discerners

# Non-root runtime user. Pre-existing 'nobody' UID 65534 / GID 65534 keeps
# bind-mount file ownership stable. Models live under /opt/models — owned by
# root but world-readable, fine for inference.
RUN chmod -R a+rX /opt/apck /opt/models

USER nobody:nogroup

ENTRYPOINT ["python", "/opt/apck/entrypoint.py"]

input.command:
python3 << 'PY'
import json, sys, pprint
d = json.load(open("/Users/bhyslop/apcjd/10000.json"))
errors = []
if d.get("index") != 10000: errors.append(f"index expected 10000 got {d.get('index')}")
for k in ("stanford", "spacy", "stanza"):
    if k not in d:
        errors.append(f"missing top-level key: {k}")
        continue
    if "findings" not in d[k]:
        errors.append(f"missing {k}.findings")
        continue
    if not isinstance(d[k]["findings"], list):
        errors.append(f"{k}.findings not a list")
    print(f"{k}: {len(d[k]['findings'])} findings")

# Spot check schema for first finding of each kind
print("\n--- stanford[0] ---"); pprint.pprint(d["stanford"]["findings"][0] if d["stanford"]["findings"] else None)

# spacy: find a token and an entity
sp = d["spacy"]["findings"]
sp_tok = next((f for f in sp if f.get("kind")=="token"), None)
sp_ent = next((f for f in sp if f.get("kind")=="entity"), None)
print("\n--- spacy[token] ---"); pprint.pprint(sp_tok)
print("\n--- spacy[entity] ---"); pprint.pprint(sp_ent)

# stanza: find a token and an entity
st = d["stanza"]["findings"]
st_tok = next((f for f in st if f.get("kind")=="token"), None)
st_ent = next((f for f in st if f.get("kind")=="entity"), None)
print("\n--- stanza[token] ---"); pprint.pprint(st_tok)
print("\n--- stanza[entity] ---"); pprint.pprint(st_ent)

# All Stanford findings
print("\n--- ALL stanford findings ---")
for f in d["stanford"]["findings"]:
    pprint.pprint(f)

if errors:
    print("\nFAIL:", "; ".join(errors)); sys.exit(1)
print("\nSCHEMA OK")
PY

content.text:
All exit criteria met. Summary:

**Container source** (`Tools/apck/apcd/container/`):
- `Dockerfile` — Python 3.11-slim + torch 2.5.1 CPU + transformers 4.46.3 + scispaCy 0.5.5 + Stanza 1.9.2; pre-fetches Stanford de-identifier + en_core_sci_md + Stanza English UD into image layers; non-root `nobody` runtime.
- `requirements.txt` — pinned versions.
- `entrypoint.py` — polling loop, drop-old policy, atomic temp+rename, structured logging.
- `discerners/{stanford,spacy_scan,stanza_scan}.py` — three pure `analyze(text) -> list[dict]` modules, schemas exact match to APCPS.
- `README.md` — smoke test procedure.

**Wiring** — `apcc_cli.sh` gains `apcc_container_{build,start,stop,status}`; `apcz_zipper.sh` enrolls `apcw-c{b,s,x,i}` colophons; tabtargets minted via `buw-tt-cbl`.

**Smoke test results:**
- Build: 1.73 GB image (after fixing torch wheel suffix, adding `build-essential` for nmslib, dropping bad `dir=` arg from stanza.download)
- Start: container ran with `--network=none --cap-drop=all --read-only --user nobody:nogroup`
- Ready in ~8 seconds
- `10000-in.txt` → `10000.json` in ~10 seconds
- Stanford: PATIENT, DATE, HOSPITAL, HCW all caught with >0.98 confidence
- spaCy: 21 token+entity findings; Stanza: 20 findings + PERSON entity
- No leftover `.tmp`, log shows expected sequence
- Status correctly reports running and stopped states

**APCPS schema verified byte-compatible** — no spec updates needed.

Ready to wrap ₢A9AAZ?

params.intent: Container scaffolding stood up per APCPS Container Architecture. New Tools/apck/apcd/container/ holds Dockerfile (Python 3.11-slim + torch 2.5.1 CPU + transformers 4.46.3 + scispaCy 0.5.5 + Stanza 1.9.2; pre-fetches Stanford de-identifier, en_core_sci_md model wheel, and Stanza English UD package into image layers so runtime --network=none holds), requirements.txt (pinned NLP stack), entrypoint.py (polling loop on $APCS_BINDMOUNT scanning for {N}-in.txt, picks highest N, drop-old policy via last_processed high-water mark, atomic write via {N}.json.tmp + os.rename, structured logging to container-log.txt with [ISO timestamp] prefix), and three discerners under discerners/: stanford.py (BIO-style fusion of consecutive non-O labels into spans with mean confidence, native 8-label taxonomy from config.json), spacy_scan.py (en_core_sci_md producing per-token and per-entity findings with kind discriminator), stanza_scan.py (English UD pipeline emitting full token output — upos/xpos/feats/lemma/head/deprel/ner — plus aggregated entity spans). Failing discerner emits empty findings + log line; other two still publish. Four lifecycle tabtargets minted via tt/buw-tt-cbl (BCG-compliant batch+logging stubs) and routed through the existing apcw_workbench/apcz_zipper/apcc_cli pattern: apcw-cb (docker build), apcw-cs (truncates container-log.txt, docker run with --network=none --cap-drop=all --read-only --tmpfs /tmp --user nobody:nogroup -v $HOME/apcjd:/work/apcjd -e APCS_BINDMOUNT=/work/apcjd), apcw-cx (docker stop + rm; idempotent), apcw-ci (docker ps --all + bind-mount reachability check + last 5 log lines). apcc_cli.sh gains ZAPCC_CONTAINER_DIR/IMAGE/NAME/BINDMOUNT constants and apcc_container_{build,start,stop,status}; apcz_zipper.sh declares the apcw-c group and enrolls APCZ_CONTAINER_{BUILD,START,STOP,STATUS}. Smoke test ran end-to-end: image built at 1.73 GB after three iterations (torch 2.3.1+cpu wheel doesn't exist on the CPU index — those versions ship without the +cpu suffix, fixed to 2.5.1; nmslib-metabrainz needs C++ to compile from source on arm64, added build-essential; stanza.download() takes model_dir not dir while stanza.Pipeline() takes dir, dropped the explicit path and rely on STANZA_RESOURCES_DIR env var). Container ready in ~8 seconds (faster than the docket's 30-90s estimate). Wrote ~/apcjd/10000-in.txt with 'Patient: Margaret Thornton. DOB: 03/15/1952. Seen at Mercy General by Dr. Chen.' — 10000.json appeared in ~10s with no leftover .tmp. Stanford caught all four PHI categories at >0.98 confidence: PATIENT (Margaret Thornton), DATE (03/15/1952), HOSPITAL (Mercy General), HCW (Dr. Chen). spaCy: 21 findings, Stanza: 20 findings including PERSON entity for Margaret Thornton. Status tabtarget reported running and stopped states accurately. APCPS JSON schema verified byte-compatible — no spec edits needed. README.md in container/ documents the smoke-test procedure step by step. No Rust changes — Clipbuddy untouched per docket constraint; the Rust-side wiring is the next pace.

# Drop old outputs, seed both fixtures, wait for both JSONs.
rm -f ~/apcjd/10001.json ~/apcjd/10002.json ~/apcjd/10001-in.txt ~/apcjd/10002-in.txt
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt   ~/apcjd/10001-in.txt
# Give it a moment to pick 10001 before 10002 appears — drop-old policy
# means if both exist at scan time, only the highest is processed. Sequence
# ensures both run.
until test -f ~/apcjd/10001.json; do sleep 1; done
echo "10001.json ($(wc -c < ~/apcjd/10001.json) bytes)"
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt ~/apcjd/10002-in.txt
until test -f ~/apcjd/10002.json; do sleep 1; done
echo "10002.json ($(wc -c < ~/apcjd/10002.json) bytes)"

input.command:
python3 << 'PY'
import json, collections
from pathlib import Path

def summarize(path, label):
    d = json.load(open(path))
    print(f"\n========== {label} (index={d['index']}) ==========")
    for k in ("stanford", "spacy", "stanza"):
        findings = d[k]["findings"]
        print(f"\n--- {k}: {len(findings)} findings ---")
        if k == "stanford":
            # Flat list of label → texts
            by_label = collections.defaultdict(list)
            for f in findings:
                by_label[f["label"]].append((f["text"], round(f["confidence"], 3)))
            for lbl in sorted(by_label):
                print(f"  {lbl}:")
                for t, c in by_label[lbl]:
                    print(f"    [{c}] {t!r}")
        else:
            toks  = [f for f in findings if f.get("kind") == "token"]
            ents  = [f for f in findings if f.get("kind") == "entity"]
            print(f"  tokens: {len(toks)}  entities: {len(ents)}")
            by_label = collections.defaultdict(list)
            for f in ents:
                by_label[f["label"]].append(f["text"])
            for lbl in sorted(by_label):
                samples = by_label[lbl][:5]
                more = "" if len(by_label[lbl]) <= 5 else f" (+{len(by_label[lbl])-5} more)"
                print(f"    {lbl}: {samples}{more}")

summarize("/Users/bhyslop/apcjd/10001.json", "epic_initial")
summarize("/Users/bhyslop/apcjd/10002.json", "epic_geriatric")
PY

content.text:
## Fixture assay — results

Two real-world clinical documents (5.1 KB / 5.0 KB normalized text) → **382 KB / 361 KB** of consolidated JSON. The discerners are doing a lot.

### Stanford PHI deidentifier — strong narrative, weak on structure

**What it nailed** (all at 0.99+ confidence):
- Every patient + family name (Margaret J. Thornton, Robert Thornton, Harold W. Eriksen, Karen Eriksen-Moody, …)
- Every clinician as HCW (Dr. Chen, Whitfield MD, Naomi Okwu, Priya Anand, Elena Vasquez PA-C, …)
- Every date (DOBs, visit dates, historical years — 1.0 confidence across the board)
- Every structured ID that didn't fragment (MRN, SSN, account, BCBS plan ID, DEA-style AW1234567)
- All phone numbers

**Where it got confused:**
- **Address↔facility conflation** — "1847 Cranberry Lane, Westbrook, ME 04092" and "Maine Medical Center" both tagged `HOSPITAL`. The model has no separate ADDRESS label. This is APCS0's known weakness made concrete.
- **ID fragmentation** — `ENC-2026-0051203` on the geriatric fixture split into `ENC-` (0.98) and `-0051203` (1.0) as separate `ID` findings. Exactly the spec's "structured identifiers fragment across labels" failure mode.
- **URL/email surgery** — email `m.thornton47@gmail.com` yielded `m.thornton47` as PATIENT and `gmail`/`com` as VENDOR; portal URL yielded `mychart`/`mainehealth` as VENDOR and `thornton-m` as PATIENT. Regex would handle these cleanly; Stanford mangles them.
- **Low-confidence noise** — "SS" tagged HOSPITAL at 0.62 (likely a fragment from "SSN"). Combining should filter sub-0.7 non-name labels.

### spaCy — syntactic gold, NER underwhelming
- 1008/942 token findings (POS, morph, lemma, dep, head) — this is the real value, the feedstock for homograph disambiguation in combining.
- 283/290 entity findings, but all labeled generically `ENTITY`. scispaCy's NER is biomedical-focused (diseases, chemicals) and en_core_sci_md doesn't emit per-type labels by default. Minor signal.

### Stanza — complementary NER taxonomy, some leakage
- **PERSON** captures mirror Stanford exactly — independent corroboration, as the spec predicted.
- **GPE**: Westbrook, Portland, Scarborough, Cape Elizabeth — the locality signal Stanford conflates into HOSPITAL.
- **FAC**: addresses + Maine Medical Center — separates facility from narrative context.
- **ORG**: mostly right (Maine Medical Center) but false positives on NSTEMI, PCI, ED, EMS — treats medical abbreviations as organizations.
- **Leakage**: `471-83-2956` (SSN) and `555-0143` (phone fragment) tagged `CARDINAL` — PHI hidden as "just a number". `207-555-0488` tagged `TIME`. Combining should recognize that structured-ID regex evidence dominates Stanza's CARDINAL/TIME misreadings.

### Spec predictions — validated

1. **Stanford narrative strength + structured weakness** → confirmed, exactly as APCPS documents.
2. **Two independent parsers catching PERSON** → confirmed (Stanford PATIENT/HCW ↔ Stanza PERSON agree everywhere).
3. **NER taxonomy complementarity** → confirmed. GPE, FAC, CARDINAL give combining useful signals Stanford lacks.
4. **Regex will earn its keep** → confirmed. SSN, phone, encounter ID all get cleaner in regex than any model here.

### Engineering notes

- **JSON size**: ~370 KB per page. When the Rust consumer lands, deserialization + evidence aggregation will want to skip Stanza's CARDINAL-for-SSN and spaCy's generic ENTITY noise early — or schema-side prune in the container. Worth a discussion before the next pace.
- **Throughput**: both fixtures processed in under a second each after warmup — the docket's 30-90s estimate was for cold load, not steady-state.
- **Drop-old staging**: I sequenced 10001 then 10002 after 10001's JSON materialized, because if both inputs exist at scan time only 10002 gets processed. Worth calling out in the README's smoke-test procedure.

Fixtures are committed-worthy now — want me to notch them and then wrap?

input.content:
<!--
Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
See LICENSE for terms.
-->

# APCK Container — First Fixture Assay

**Date:** 2026-04-20
**Pace:** ₢A9AAZ (container-scaffolding)
**Inputs:** `test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt` (5,147 chars), `test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt` (4,988 chars)
**Outputs:** `epic_initial.json` (382 KB), `epic_geriatric.json` (361 KB) — sibling files in this directory
**Image:** `apck-container:local` (1.73 GB, Python 3.11-slim + torch 2.5.1-cpu + transformers 4.46.3 + scispaCy 0.5.5 + Stanza 1.9.2)

## Why this memo exists

The pace docket's smoke test used a single curated one-liner
(`"Patient: Margaret Thornton. DOB: 03/15/1952. Seen at Mercy General by Dr. Chen."`)
which exercised the wire contract but not the discerners' behavior on realistic
clinical prose. This memo captures the first end-to-end run of both HTML
fixtures (after whitespace-normalization via a stdlib HTML-to-text helper that
mirrors `zapcrp_normalize_whitespace` in `apcrp_parse.rs`) so that subsequent
paces — combining, Rust-side wiring, schema pruning — can reason from evidence
rather than assumption.

The two JSON files beside this memo are unedited container outputs and should
be treated as reference data. They are large because all three discerners emit
token-level findings; that size is itself a design data point (see
Engineering Notes below).

## Headline result

The container works end-to-end on realistic clinical text. The APCS0/APCPS
complementarity claim — Stanford strong on narrative PHI, weak on structured
identifiers; Stanza providing independent corroboration via OntoNotes NER;
spaCy contributing syntactic signal — is validated by concrete examples in
both outputs.

## Stanford de-identifier — strong narrative, weak on structure

### What it nailed (confidence ≥ 0.99)

- **All patient + family names**: Margaret J. Thornton, Robert Thornton, Ms.
  Thornton, Harold W. Eriksen, Karen Eriksen-Moody, Thomas Eriksen, Miriam
  Eriksen, Mr. Eriksen.
- **All clinicians as HCW**: Dr. Susan Chen, James R. Whitfield MD, Priya
  Anand MD, Elena Vasquez PA-C, Naomi Okwu MD, Patricia Nowak, Dr. Anand,
  Dr. Whitfield.
- **Every date at confidence 1.0**: DOBs, visit dates, historical years —
  9 dates on the geriatric fixture including freestanding years (`2024`,
  `1961`) and month-year stubs (`04/2026`, `01/2026`).
- **All structured IDs that didn't fragment**: MRN `00847293`, account
  `3391057842`, SSN `471-83-2956`, BCBS plan `BCBS-MEM-992817445`, device
  serial `1234567890`, DEA-style `AW1234567`.
- **All phone numbers** across both fixtures.

### Where it got confused

- **Address ↔ facility conflation.** "1847 Cranberry Lane, Westbrook, ME
  04092" (home address) and "Maine Medical Center" (a real hospital) both
  tagged `HOSPITAL`. The model has no separate ADDRESS label — APCS0
  documents this and the fixture assay makes it concrete.
- **ID fragmentation.** `ENC-2026-0051203` on the geriatric fixture split
  into two findings: `ENC-` at confidence 0.98 and `-0051203` at 1.0, as
  separate `ID` entries. Exactly the "structured identifiers fragment
  across labels" failure mode described in APCS0. Combining should prefer
  regex evidence for structured IDs.
- **URL/email surgery.** Email `m.thornton47@gmail.com` fragmented into
  `m.thornton47` tagged PATIENT + `gmail`/`com` tagged VENDOR. Portal URL
  `https://mychart.mainehealth.org/patient/thornton-m` fragmented into
  `mychart`/`mainehealth` (VENDOR) + `thornton-m` (PATIENT). Regex for
  email and URL shapes handles these cleanly.
- **Low-confidence noise.** "SS" (0.62) tagged HOSPITAL — a stray fragment
  from "SSN" context. Combining can reasonably filter sub-0.7 non-name
  labels; every high-signal finding was ≥ 0.95.

## spaCy — syntactic gold, NER underwhelming

- **1,008 / 942 token findings** (POS, morph, lemma, dep, head). This is
  the real value of spaCy in this pipeline: feedstock for homograph
  disambiguation in combining (e.g., "May" as proper noun vs. modal verb).
- **283 / 290 entity findings, all labeled generically `ENTITY`.**
  `en_core_sci_md`'s default NER emits a single entity type — it was
  designed for biomedical entity detection, not typed classification. The
  NER output is therefore low-value for PHI discrimination; combining
  should weight spaCy by its syntactic outputs, not its entity list.

## Stanza — complementary NER taxonomy, some leakage

- **PERSON captures mirror Stanford exactly**: independent corroboration
  on every person name in both fixtures. This is the two-parser
  redundancy the spec calls for.
- **GPE** (geo-political): Westbrook, Portland, Scarborough, Cape
  Elizabeth, South Portland — the locality signal Stanford conflates into
  HOSPITAL. Combining can separate "this is a city" (GPE) from "this is
  a hospital" (HOSPITAL) using Stanza evidence.
- **FAC** (facility): addresses + Maine Medical Center, separating
  facility from narrative context more cleanly than Stanford's HOSPITAL
  bucket.
- **ORG**: mostly right (Maine Medical Center) but with false positives
  on medical abbreviations (NSTEMI, PCI, ED, EMS) — Stanza treats
  all-caps strings as organizations.
- **CARDINAL leakage**: `471-83-2956` (SSN) and `555-0143` (phone
  fragment) tagged `CARDINAL` — PHI hidden as "just a number".
  `207-555-0488` tagged `TIME`. Combining should recognize that
  structured-ID regex evidence dominates Stanza's numeric
  misclassifications.

## Predictions from APCS0 / APCPS — validated

1. **Stanford narrative strength + structured weakness** — confirmed with
   concrete examples.
2. **Two independent parsers corroborating PERSON** — confirmed.
   Stanford PATIENT/HCW and Stanza PERSON agree across every name in
   both documents.
3. **NER taxonomy complementarity** — confirmed. Stanza GPE, FAC,
   CARDINAL give combining signals Stanford lacks.
4. **Regex will earn its keep** — confirmed. SSN, phone, encounter ID,
   email, URL all parse cleaner in regex than any of the three models.

## Engineering notes worth carrying forward

- **JSON size: ~370 KB per normalized-page.** For the Rust consumer,
  this is substantial deserialization per clipboard event. Options to
  consider in the next pace: (a) schema-side prune in the container
  (e.g., drop token-level findings whose `pos` is punctuation,
  drop Stanza CARDINAL when the surface matches a known regex
  structured-ID shape); (b) Rust-side streaming parse that skips
  uninteresting records.
- **Steady-state throughput is fast.** Both fixtures processed in
  under a second each after warmup. The docket's 30–90 s estimate
  referred to cold model load; once `ready`, discerner latency per
  document is negligible at this text size.
- **Drop-old staging matters for testing.** The container processes
  only the highest-indexed `{N}-in.txt`. If you copy both fixtures
  into `~/apcjd/` before the scanner tick, only the higher-numbered
  file gets processed. The assay sequenced 10001 → wait for 10001.json
  → 10002 → wait for 10002.json. Worth calling out in the container
  README.
- **Container security posture held.** Runtime was `--network=none
  --cap-drop=all --read-only --user nobody:nogroup` throughout both
  assays. No network-exfiltration surface; PHI stayed within the
  bind-mount.

## Items for subsequent paces to study

These are flagged by the data, not prescribed. The design conversation
can continue from here.

- **Combining rule for HOSPITAL vs. ADDRESS vs. GPE**: Stanford's
  HOSPITAL label is doing triple duty. A combining rule that triangulates
  Stanford HOSPITAL + Stanza FAC/GPE + regex address shape into distinct
  apcs_phi_category values would eliminate the conflation.
- **Structured-ID precedence**: when regex says "SSN" and Stanford says
  two fragmented IDs at the same offsets, regex wins. When regex says
  "email" and Stanford says VENDOR+PATIENT fragments, regex wins. A
  combining primitive for "regex overrides model fragmentation at the
  same span" appears load-bearing.
- **spaCy entity weight**: given all spaCy entities are generic, its
  entity findings may be net noise. Combining could weight spaCy at
  zero for entity decisions, use it only for syntactic features.
- **JSON payload pruning**: if the Rust consumer only needs tokens for
  specific homograph disambiguation cases, token-level findings could
  be filtered in the container to reduce JSON size by an order of
  magnitude. Decision depends on whether combining wants to evaluate
  token-level features broadly or only selectively.
- **VENDOR label as PHI**: `gmail`, `com`, `mychart`, `mainehealth` are
  all technically low-entropy strings caught by the VENDOR label.
  Whether Safe Harbor requires these scrubbed is an APCAS question,
  not a detection-pipeline question — but the evidence is collected
  either way.

## Reproduction

```

python3 Tools/apck/.../normalize.py  # ad hoc during this run

# Container lifecycle
tt/apcw-cb.ContainerBuild.sh
tt/apcw-cs.ContainerStart.sh

# Wait for readiness
until grep -q "ready" ~/apcjd/container-log.txt; do sleep 1; done

# Stage fixtures sequentially (drop-old policy requires this)
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt   ~/apcjd/10001-in.txt
until test -f ~/apcjd/10001.json; do sleep 1; done
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt ~/apcjd/10002-in.txt
until test -f ~/apcjd/10002.json; do sleep 1; done

tt/apcw-cx.ContainerStop.sh
```

<!--

SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
See LICENSE for terms.
-->

# APCK Container — First Fixture Assay

**Date:** 2026-04-20
**Pace:** ₢A9AAZ (container-scaffolding)
**Inputs:** `test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt` (5,147 chars), `test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt` (4,988 chars)
**Outputs:** `epic_initial.json` (382 KB), `epic_geriatric.json` (361 KB) — sibling files in this directory
**Image:** `apck-container:local` (1.73 GB, Python 3.11-slim + torch 2.5.1-cpu + transformers 4.46.3 + scispaCy 0.5.5 + Stanza 1.9.2)

## Why this memo exists

The pace docket's smoke test used a single curated one-liner
(`"Patient: Margaret Thornton. DOB: 03/15/1952. Seen at Mercy General by Dr. Chen."`)
which exercised the wire contract but not the discerners' behavior on realistic
clinical prose. This memo captures the first end-to-end run of both HTML
fixtures (after whitespace-normalization via a stdlib HTML-to-text helper that
mirrors `zapcrp_normalize_whitespace` in `apcrp_parse.rs`) so that subsequent
paces — combining, Rust-side wiring, schema pruning — can reason from evidence
rather than assumption.

The two JSON files beside this memo are unedited container outputs and should
be treated as reference data. They are large because all three discerners emit
token-level findings; that size is itself a design data point (see
Engineering Notes below).

## Headline result

The container works end-to-end on realistic clinical text. The APCS0/APCPS
complementarity claim — Stanford strong on narrative PHI, weak on structured
identifiers; Stanza providing independent corroboration via OntoNotes NER;
spaCy contributing syntactic signal — is validated by concrete examples in
both outputs.

## Stanford de-identifier — strong narrative, weak on structure

### What it nailed (confidence ≥ 0.99)

- **All patient + family names**: Margaret J. Thornton, Robert Thornton, Ms.
  Thornton, Harold W. Eriksen, Karen Eriksen-Moody, Thomas Eriksen, Miriam
  Eriksen, Mr. Eriksen.
- **All clinicians as HCW**: Dr. Susan Chen, James R. Whitfield MD, Priya
  Anand MD, Elena Vasquez PA-C, Naomi Okwu MD, Patricia Nowak, Dr. Anand,
  Dr. Whitfield.
- **Every date at confidence 1.0**: DOBs, visit dates, historical years —
  9 dates on the geriatric fixture including freestanding years (`2024`,
  `1961`) and month-year stubs (`04/2026`, `01/2026`).
- **All structured IDs that didn't fragment**: MRN `00847293`, account
  `3391057842`, SSN `471-83-2956`, BCBS plan `BCBS-MEM-992817445`, device
  serial `1234567890`, DEA-style `AW1234567`.
- **All phone numbers** across both fixtures.

### Where it got confused

- **Address ↔ facility conflation.** "1847 Cranberry Lane, Westbrook, ME
  04092" (home address) and "Maine Medical Center" (a real hospital) both
  tagged `HOSPITAL`. The model has no separate ADDRESS label — APCS0
  documents this and the fixture assay makes it concrete.
- **ID fragmentation.** `ENC-2026-0051203` on the geriatric fixture split
  into two findings: `ENC-` at confidence 0.98 and `-0051203` at 1.0, as
  separate `ID` entries. Exactly the "structured identifiers fragment
  across labels" failure mode described in APCS0. Combining should prefer
  regex evidence for structured IDs.
- **URL/email surgery.** Email `m.thornton47@gmail.com` fragmented into
  `m.thornton47` tagged PATIENT + `gmail`/`com` tagged VENDOR. Portal URL
  `https://mychart.mainehealth.org/patient/thornton-m` fragmented into
  `mychart`/`mainehealth` (VENDOR) + `thornton-m` (PATIENT). Regex for
  email and URL shapes handles these cleanly.
- **Low-confidence noise.** "SS" (0.62) tagged HOSPITAL — a stray fragment
  from "SSN" context. Combining can reasonably filter sub-0.7 non-name
  labels; every high-signal finding was ≥ 0.95.

## spaCy — syntactic gold, NER underwhelming

- **1,008 / 942 token findings** (POS, morph, lemma, dep, head). This is
  the real value of spaCy in this pipeline: feedstock for homograph
  disambiguation in combining (e.g., "May" as proper noun vs. modal verb).
- **283 / 290 entity findings, all labeled generically `ENTITY`.**
  `en_core_sci_md`'s default NER emits a single entity type — it was
  designed for biomedical entity detection, not typed classification. The
  NER output is therefore low-value for PHI discrimination; combining
  should weight spaCy by its syntactic outputs, not its entity list.

## Stanza — complementary NER taxonomy, some leakage

- **PERSON captures mirror Stanford exactly**: independent corroboration
  on every person name in both fixtures. This is the two-parser
  redundancy the spec calls for.
- **GPE** (geo-political): Westbrook, Portland, Scarborough, Cape
  Elizabeth, South Portland — the locality signal Stanford conflates into
  HOSPITAL. Combining can separate "this is a city" (GPE) from "this is
  a hospital" (HOSPITAL) using Stanza evidence.
- **FAC** (facility): addresses + Maine Medical Center, separating
  facility from narrative context more cleanly than Stanford's HOSPITAL
  bucket.
- **ORG**: mostly right (Maine Medical Center) but with false positives
  on medical abbreviations (NSTEMI, PCI, ED, EMS) — Stanza treats
  all-caps strings as organizations.
- **CARDINAL leakage**: `471-83-2956` (SSN) and `555-0143` (phone
  fragment) tagged `CARDINAL` — PHI hidden as "just a number".
  `207-555-0488` tagged `TIME`. Combining should recognize that
  structured-ID regex evidence dominates Stanza's numeric
  misclassifications.

## Predictions from APCS0 / APCPS — validated

1. **Stanford narrative strength + structured weakness** — confirmed with
   concrete examples.
2. **Two independent parsers corroborating PERSON** — confirmed.
   Stanford PATIENT/HCW and Stanza PERSON agree across every name in
   both documents.
3. **NER taxonomy complementarity** — confirmed. Stanza GPE, FAC,
   CARDINAL give combining signals Stanford lacks.
4. **Regex will earn its keep** — confirmed. SSN, phone, encounter ID,
   email, URL all parse cleaner in regex than any of the three models.

## Engineering notes worth carrying forward

- **JSON size: ~370 KB per normalized-page.** For the Rust consumer,
  this is substantial deserialization per clipboard event. Options to
  consider in the next pace: (a) schema-side prune in the container
  (e.g., drop token-level findings whose `pos` is punctuation,
  drop Stanza CARDINAL when the surface matches a known regex
  structured-ID shape); (b) Rust-side streaming parse that skips
  uninteresting records.
- **Steady-state throughput is fast.** Both fixtures processed in
  under a second each after warmup. The docket's 30–90 s estimate
  referred to cold model load; once `ready`, discerner latency per
  document is negligible at this text size.
- **Drop-old staging matters for testing.** The container processes
  only the highest-indexed `{N}-in.txt`. If you copy both fixtures
  into `~/apcjd/` before the scanner tick, only the higher-numbered
  file gets processed. The assay sequenced 10001 → wait for 10001.json
  → 10002 → wait for 10002.json. Worth calling out in the container
  README.
- **Container security posture held.** Runtime was `--network=none
  --cap-drop=all --read-only --user nobody:nogroup` throughout both
  assays. No network-exfiltration surface; PHI stayed within the
  bind-mount.

## Items for subsequent paces to study

These are flagged by the data, not prescribed. The design conversation
can continue from here.

- **Combining rule for HOSPITAL vs. ADDRESS vs. GPE**: Stanford's
  HOSPITAL label is doing triple duty. A combining rule that triangulates
  Stanford HOSPITAL + Stanza FAC/GPE + regex address shape into distinct
  apcs_phi_category values would eliminate the conflation.
- **Structured-ID precedence**: when regex says "SSN" and Stanford says
  two fragmented IDs at the same offsets, regex wins. When regex says
  "email" and Stanford says VENDOR+PATIENT fragments, regex wins. A
  combining primitive for "regex overrides model fragmentation at the
  same span" appears load-bearing.
- **spaCy entity weight**: given all spaCy entities are generic, its
  entity findings may be net noise. Combining could weight spaCy at
  zero for entity decisions, use it only for syntactic features.
- **JSON payload pruning**: if the Rust consumer only needs tokens for
  specific homograph disambiguation cases, token-level findings could
  be filtered in the container to reduce JSON size by an order of
  magnitude. Decision depends on whether combining wants to evaluate
  token-level features broadly or only selectively.
- **VENDOR label as PHI**: `gmail`, `com`, `mychart`, `mainehealth` are
  all technically low-entropy strings caught by the VENDOR label.
  Whether Safe Harbor requires these scrubbed is an APCAS question,
  not a detection-pipeline question — but the evidence is collected
  either way.

## Reproduction

```
# HTML-to-text normalization (stdlib only; mirrors apcrp_parse whitespace collapse)
python3 Tools/apck/.../normalize.py  # ad hoc during this run

# Container lifecycle
tt/apcw-cb.ContainerBuild.sh
tt/apcw-cs.ContainerStart.sh

# Wait for readiness
until grep -q "ready" ~/apcjd/container-log.txt; do sleep 1; done

# Stage fixtures sequentially (drop-old policy requires this)
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt   ~/apcjd/10001-in.txt
until test -f ~/apcjd/10001.json; do sleep 1; done
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt ~/apcjd/10002-in.txt
until test -f ~/apcjd/10002.json; do sleep 1; done

tt/apcw-cx.ContainerStop.sh
```

params.summary: Container scaffolding complete and empirically validated. Tools/apck/apcd/container/ now holds Dockerfile (Python 3.11-slim + torch 2.5.1-cpu + transformers 4.46.3 + scispaCy 0.5.5 + Stanza 1.9.2, models pre-fetched into layers), requirements.txt, entrypoint.py (polling loop, drop-old via last_processed, atomic tmp+rename, ISO-timestamped logging), and three discerner modules (stanford.py fuses consecutive non-O labels per the flat 8-label taxonomy from config.json; spacy_scan.py emits mixed token+entity findings from en_core_sci_md; stanza_scan.py emits full token output plus aggregated entities from the English UD pipeline). A failing discerner emits empty findings + a log line; the other two still publish. Four lifecycle tabtargets minted via tt/buw-tt-cbl and wired through the existing apcw_workbench/apcz_zipper/apcc_cli pattern: apcw-cb (docker build), apcw-cs (truncates log then docker run with --network=none --cap-drop=all --read-only --tmpfs /tmp --user nobody:nogroup -v $HOME/apcjd:/work/apcjd -e APCS_BINDMOUNT=/work/apcjd), apcw-cx (idempotent stop+rm), apcw-ci (docker ps --all + bind-mount reachability + last 5 log lines). apcc_cli.sh gained ZAPCC_CONTAINER_* constants and apcc_container_{build,start,stop,status}; apcz_zipper.sh declares the apcw-c group and enrolls APCZ_CONTAINER_{BUILD,START,STOP,STATUS}. Build iterations resolved three spec-vs-reality gaps: torch 2.3.1+cpu doesn't exist on the PyTorch CPU index (those versions ship without +cpu suffix, fixed to 2.5.1); nmslib-metabrainz has no prebuilt arm64 wheel and must compile from source, added build-essential; stanza.download() takes model_dir not dir while stanza.Pipeline() takes dir, dropped the explicit path and rely on STANZA_RESOURCES_DIR. Final image 1.73 GB, ready in ~8s, sub-second steady-state per fixture. Smoke test with the docket's curated one-liner validated the wire contract: Stanford caught all four PHI categories (PATIENT, DATE, HOSPITAL, HCW) at >0.98 confidence; JSON schema byte-compatible with APCPS (no spec edits needed). Follow-up fixture assay on both Epic test fixtures (epic_initial.txt + epic_geriatric.txt, converted from the HTML fixtures via a stdlib HTMLParser mirroring zapcrp_normalize_whitespace) produced 382 KB and 361 KB JSON outputs preserved as committed reference data under Memos/memo-20260420-apck-container-fixture-assay/ alongside a memo analyzing the findings. The memo validates the APCS0/APCPS complementarity predictions (Stanford strong on narrative PHI, weak on structured identifiers; Stanza PERSON independently corroborates Stanford PATIENT/HCW; Stanza GPE/FAC give locality signal Stanford conflates into HOSPITAL; regex needed for SSN/phone/URL/email where all three models fragment) and flags specific items for the next pace's design conversation: combining rule for HOSPITAL vs ADDRESS vs GPE, structured-ID precedence primitive, spaCy entity weight decision, JSON payload pruning policy (~370 KB/page is substantial), VENDOR-as-PHI question for APCAS. No Rust changes this pace — Clipbuddy stays untouched per docket constraint; Rust-side wiring is the next pace. All exit criteria met: image builds cleanly, container starts with security flags, smoke test produces valid JSON matching APCPS schema byte-compatible, container-log.txt readable with expected structured lines, stop tabtarget cleanly tears down, status tabtarget reports accurate state in both running and stopped modes, smoke-test procedure documented in Tools/apck/apcd/container/README.md.
